title: Ask for a visible reasoning structure

spec.title: Ask for a visible reasoning structure
spec.notes_for_generator: Analyse a business scenario. Teach the learner to ask for factors, assumptions, trade-offs, and evidence needed before conclusions. Avoid hidden chain-of-thought language and avoid implying the model's reasoning is proof.

content:
SUBJECT: AI Gym Day 4: Ask it to think, not just answer

Most people use AI like a search engine. They ask a question, get an answer, and move on. That's leaving most of the value on the table.

TODAY'S WORKOUT
───────────────
🔍 ANALYSIS | 🔵 STEADY STATE | ⏱ 12–18 min

THE BRIEF:
Your company is considering stopping free parking for staff to reduce costs and encourage sustainable commuting. Leadership wants to understand the decision before committing. Your job is to use AI to map out the landscape of this problem — not to get a recommendation, but to surface the factors, assumptions, and gaps that matter before anyone draws a conclusion.

YOUR REPS:

1. Start by asking for structure, not answers. Pick a business decision relevant to your own work — it can be the parking scenario above or something from your own context (anonymised if needed). Then ask the AI to identify the key factors that would need to be understood before a recommendation could be made. Try something like: "Before we could decide whether [describe the decision], what factors would need to be understood? Break these down by category." Your job is to write a version of that prompt with enough detail about the scenario that the AI has something real to work with.

2. Look at what came back. Now push on the weakest part of it. Pick one factor the AI listed that seems thin, obvious, or too vague — and ask it to go deeper. Ask what assumptions are baked into that factor, and what evidence you'd need to test those assumptions. You're not accepting the first pass as complete; you're treating it as a first draft of a map.

3. Ask the AI to identify what it doesn't know. Send a follow-up prompt asking it to flag where its analysis relies on assumptions it can't verify — and what the consequences would be if those assumptions turned out to be wrong. Then read what it says critically. Note: this isn't proof the AI has "checked itself." It's a useful prompt technique for surfacing gaps, but you still need to apply your own judgement to what comes back.

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
Your output is good if:
• The factors list covers at least three distinct dimensions (e.g. financial, human, operational) — not just a flat list of bullet points that all feel the same
• The second rep produced something meaningfully more specific than the first — a sign the follow-up prompt did real work
• The assumptions the AI surfaces in rep 3 are genuinely testable, not just vague hedges like "it depends on the context"
• At least one thing in the output surprises you, prompts a question, or makes you want to investigate further

💡 COACHING NOTE:
Asking for factors and assumptions before conclusions is one of the most reliable ways to make AI output more useful. When you ask "what should we do?", the AI fills in the gaps with plausible-sounding defaults. When you ask "what would we need to know first?", it maps the problem instead — which is often more valuable than a premature answer. This technique transfers well beyond analysis: use it in research, planning, and any situation where you're tempted to skip straight to the output. The output is still a draft. Your job is to decide whether the map it's drawn is the right one.

──────────

Three days in. You're already prompting differently to most people. The 90-Day AI Habit picks up where the free block leaves off — more depth, more practice, more outputs you can actually use. Join solo, bring a small group, or ask your employer to cover it: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/

──────────
Use only material you are allowed to share with your chosen LLM. Anonymise anything sensitive.
Share the programme: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/
Found this useful? You can leave a tip: https://ko-fi.com/theaihabit
review_notes: Clarified that AI Habit focuses on chat-based large language models (LLMs), while using AI as public shorthand.
Kept exercises model-agnostic and usable with any text-based LLM or approved work tool.
Reworked research/reasoning language to avoid over-trusting model confidence or hidden chain-of-thought.
Reframed AI Habit as a grounded free 14-day starter block with a paid continuation for deeper 60-90 day habit-building, rather than a validation experiment.

Positioned the paid continuation as The 90-Day AI Habit: a structured applied-LLM programme covering writing/editing, strategy/decision-making, research/assessment, management/delegation, personal productivity, and practical outputs.
Simplified the 90-day commercial route: join individually, bring a small group for a discounted group subscription, or use the workplace launch pack to get an employer to pay.
Added ethical upgrade nudges to Days 3, 7, and 10: endowed progress, career identity, and momentum protection for The 90-Day AI Habit.
Mechanical repair pass: normalised workout badge line after scheduler verification.
Regenerated on 2026-06-05 because original scheduled source was missing core workout sections.
